label: Pass at K
definition: The standard metric for code generation: the probability that at least one of k sampled solutions to a problem passes its tests. Correctness is decided by running the code, an objective executable check rather than a judged or reference-matched one, which makes it far less gameable, and the k captures a property unique to generative models, their usefulness under sampling (coverage versus single-shot reliability).

body.text: Pass at k, written pass@k, is the dominant metric for evaluating models that generate code. For a given programming problem the model samples k candidate solutions, and the problem counts as solved if at least one of those k candidates passes all of the problem's unit tests. The reported score is the fraction of problems solved in this sense, estimated over a benchmark such as HumanEval or MBPP. Its defining feature is that correctness is decided by running the code against tests, an objective, executable check, rather than by comparing text to a reference or asking a model to judge.

It matters because executable verification sidesteps the deepest weakness of most other metrics, the keeper. There is no partial credit and no ambiguity: the tests pass or they do not, so the signal is far less gameable than overlap scores or even llm-as-judge verdicts. The k in pass@k also captures a property unique and central to generative models: their usefulness under sampling. A model that solves a problem one time in ten is genuinely valuable in a workflow where a developer or an agent can run several attempts and keep the one that works, and pass@k makes that practical value measurable in a way that a single greedy attempt cannot.

Computing it correctly requires care, because the naive estimator is biased and high-variance. The standard approach, from the Codex paper that popularized the metric, is to generate a larger number n of samples per problem (with n much greater than k), count how many of the n pass, and then compute the unbiased combinatorial expectation that a random subset of size k contains at least one passing sample. This estimator both reduces variance and yields a stable number for any k up to n. Pass@1 measures single-shot reliability and is the figure most often quoted for everyday use, while higher k like pass@10 or pass@100 measure the model's coverage, how often a correct solution exists somewhere in its sampling distribution even if it is rarely the first guess.

Pass at k has clear limitations. Its honesty is only as good as the test suite: passing a weak or incomplete set of tests certifies code that is subtly wrong, so the metric measures "passes these tests," not "is correct." Raising k inflates the score by allowing more attempts, which can mask poor single-shot reliability and rewards a model that is merely diverse rather than dependable. The metric applies only to tasks with an executable oracle, code, theorem proving, tool-use with checkable outcomes, and cannot grade open-ended generation at all. And like every public benchmark it is exposed to benchmark-contamination, since the canonical problem sets are old, widely mirrored, and likely present in training corpora.

Pass at k is the leading example of evaluation by executable verification, the most objective branch of the field and a sharp contrast to the answer-key matching of mmlu and the subjective scoring of llm-as-judge. Its coverage-versus-reliability framing (high k versus pass@1) echoes the precision-and-recall trade-off, and its resistance to gaming is exactly why verifiable, test-checked tasks are prized wherever benchmark-contamination has eroded trust in static benchmarks.
